provenance: Curated demonstration subset. Severity classifications and descriptions follow DDInter 2.0 categories and openFDA drug-label warnings. The loader accepts any dataset in this schema — a full DDInter 2.0 export can be dropped in without code changes. NOT a complete interaction database.

interactions.description: Concurrent use significantly increases bleeding risk through combined anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects.
NSAIDs increase bleeding risk with warfarin and may cause serious gastrointestinal bleeding.
NSAIDs increase bleeding risk with warfarin and may cause serious gastrointestinal bleeding.
Fluconazole inhibits warfarin metabolism (CYP2C9), raising INR and bleeding risk; dose adjustment and INR monitoring required.
Amiodarone potentiates warfarin's anticoagulant effect; warfarin dose typically needs 30-50% reduction.
Sulfamethoxazole inhibits warfarin metabolism and displaces it from protein binding, markedly increasing bleeding risk.
PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates can cause severe, refractory hypotension. Concurrent use is contraindicated.
Combined serotonergic activity raises the risk of serotonin syndrome and lowers seizure threshold.
Combined serotonergic activity raises the risk of serotonin syndrome; fluoxetine also inhibits tramadol's conversion to its active metabolite.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, increasing tizanidine exposure up to 10-fold with severe hypotension and sedation. Contraindicated.
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 activation of clopidogrel, reducing its antiplatelet effect.
Clarithromycin strongly inhibits CYP3A4, greatly increasing simvastatin levels and rhabdomyolysis risk. Contraindicated.
Verapamil increases simvastatin exposure; simvastatin dose must not exceed 10 mg daily with verapamil.
Clarithromycin increases atorvastatin exposure and myopathy risk; use lowest necessary dose.
Amiodarone reduces digoxin clearance, roughly doubling serum digoxin; digoxin dose should be halved with level monitoring.
Verapamil increases digoxin levels by 50-75%, risking digoxin toxicity.
ACE inhibitor with potassium-sparing diuretic risks serious hyperkalemia, especially with renal impairment.
ACE inhibitors reduce potassium excretion; supplemental potassium risks hyperkalemia.
NSAIDs blunt the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and combined use stresses renal function.
NSAIDs reduce methotrexate clearance, risking severe methotrexate toxicity including myelosuppression.
NSAIDs reduce methotrexate clearance, risking severe methotrexate toxicity including myelosuppression.
Combined antifolate effects can cause severe pancytopenia. Avoid concurrent use.
Thiazides can raise blood glucose and mildly oppose metformin's effect; monitor glycemic control.
Fluconazole may increase tramadol exposure via CYP3A4/2D6 inhibition, increasing adverse-effect risk.
Reduced gastric acidity impairs levothyroxine absorption; monitor TSH and separate dosing.
Ibuprofen can block aspirin's antiplatelet effect when taken before it, and combined use increases GI bleeding risk.
Fluconazole inhibits glipizide metabolism (CYP2C9), risking severe hypoglycemia.

penicillin.note: Penicillin allergy contraindicates penicillins; ~1-2% cross-reactivity with cephalosporins warrants caution.

sulfa.note: Sulfonamide antibiotic allergy contraindicates sulfamethoxazole-containing products.

aspirin.note: Aspirin hypersensitivity frequently cross-reacts with other NSAIDs.
